entries.text: You rest against the post. The ache dulls; the light through the slats shifts.

The season turns, and some of your rice has spoiled in the store. (−958 rice)
You rest against the post. The ache dulls; the light through the slats shifts.
You rest against the post. The ache dulls; the light through the slats shifts.
The estate gate and its swept forecourt — the ground the whole house turns on.
The grain-store where you woke — dark, dry, close with the smell of old rice.
The wolf comes out of the dark among the rice-sacks. You swing the pole, miss, swing again — and somehow, more luck than skill, it bolts bleeding into the night. You are alive. You should not be.

Kihei the drillmaster finds you shaking by the stores. He says nothing for a long moment. Then: "You lived. That's the only talent that matters in the end. Come to the yard at dawn — I'll teach you the rest."

The drill yard behind the omoya, first light. You've stood over the grain-store wolf's carcass; word travels. A hard-faced man is already there, a bokken in each hand — and he throws you one without a word of greeting.
"So. You put down the thing that had the run of our stores. Farmers don't do that. There's a soldier in you under the farmhand — I've watched it a week and I'm done pretending I haven't."
entries.speaker: Kihei

entries.text: "You're gate-watch now: a weapon, a yard to train in, and the estate's defence on your shoulders — pests, beasts, and the masterless men who drift down the woodlot road. Keep a field-guide of what you face; a soldier who knows his enemy outlives one who doesn't."
entries.speaker: Kihei

entries.text: "Show me how to end a fight fast."
entries.speaker: You

entries.text: "Fast, he says. Fast gets you a spear in the back. But there's fire in it — we'll aim it before it burns you."
entries.speaker: Kihei

entries.text: The drill yard opens to you — a place to train, and foes that must be met.
You begin to keep a bestiary — a field-guide of the beasts of the estate, filling in as you face each one.

Standing in the drill yard, you begin to grasp how a house like this is truly weighed — not by its rice alone, but by its arms, its lands and coin, its standing before the lord, and the worth of its name. That reckoning sits far above a gate-watch. For now, you only see that it is there.
A dry corner of the grain-store is kept back for you now — a straw mat, a bowl, a place to lay your head. "A place here is yours," the old man said. Small, but yours to keep.
You store 120 rice safe in the kura storehouse.
The estate gate and its swept forecourt — the ground the whole house turns on.
Terraced paddies stepping down the slope — the rice that keeps the house fed.
The crop-raiding monkey overcomes you; you limp home badly used. (HP 1→1) You drop 1715 rice in the rout. Eat and mend before you take the field again.
